meta.title: Generating Alpha: The Digital Asset Hedge Fund Landscape

transcript:
Paulo Baia: So hello everyone, thank you very much for being here. Uh, my name is Paulo Baia, I'm the Head of Research at Exponential Age Asset Management. And we're here today to talk about like how to, you know, create outsized returns uh, in the digital space, in the liquid digital space. With me here today I have two old friends, I've known these guys for a long time, and uh, we're going to try to uh, you know, give you an idea of what we do. In my case as al— as capital allocators. In my case, I just find the best funds to give money to, and in their case, they find the best tokens, the best investments, they do the research and uh, provide you uh, and everybody else who's invested with uh, outsized returns. So, briefly, we don't have a lot of time, so uh, to my right I have Justin Bons. Justin is the founder and CIO of Cyber Capital. Perhaps the oldest uh, crypto fund based in Europe, they're based in Amsterdam. He's been doing this since 2016. Prior to that, he was an early Bitcoiner. Uh, next to him is Sam, uh, Sam Andrew. Sam it is Portfolio Manager and the Director of Research at North Island Ventures. It's a firm based in New York City, they run two VC funds and one liquid fund that Sam is responsible for. Prior to that, he had a very uh, successful blog uh, on uh, in Substack, writing research on crypto, and that's how I met him uh, years and years ago. So, uh, let's see how we tell this audience how we find outsized returns in the, in the crypto space. So let's start with you uh, uh, Sam. Can you describe to us like what your investment strategy is? You know, I mentioned you uh, uh, have heavy research uh, and uh, look at, what time horizons you look at to invest? What benchmarks do you compare your, your fund to? Uh, if I go to North Island uh, website, there I can read that we are entering the Golden Age of Crypto Adoption. So how do you factor that Golden Age of Crypto Adoption into your investment strategy?

Sam Andrew: Okay. Hey everyone. Um, so what we do, we're thesis-driven investors. That means we do in-depth, independent research to both surface and underwrite uh, investments. What we invest in is public crypto assets. So that means assets like Bitcoin, like SOL, on down to smaller market cap tokens, as well as crypto-linked equities and derivatives. And we think of ourselves as absolute return. Pretty much anywhere we can develop an edge in our research. Usually that has to do with some sort of technological transformation, misunderstanding particular, um, where we can develop a variant view, uh, that drives a lot of our thinking. Uh, I'd say we're long bias, uh, given where we are in the development of the industry, having a long bias is important. Um, but we also do manage our risk. Um, crypto is highly cyclical, and so we'll adjust our exposure uh, to try and blunt the effects of some of that cyclicality. We also overlay some yield strategies on idle cash we may have or ways we can amplify the returns of our underlying positions. And I think maybe to your question of how we generate outsized returns, it really comes from two factors. Factor number one is asset selection. That means being invested in category leaders. That comes from our in-depth research that we do. That's both kind of top-down thematic, there are various themes and I can elaborate on them, uh, that we want to be invested in, and bottoms-up. And so bottoms-up assesses which is the best way to express those themes across a variety of different types of assets. And the second way we generate returns is risk management. Uh, I think risk management is critically important and probably something that is maybe a little overlooked in crypto. Um, there are real cycles in crypto and understanding kind of where we are in the cycle and kind of blunting the effects of that is critically important. And that comes from portfolio construction, of understanding how much risk is in the portfolio relative to kind of where we are in that cycle. And then secondly is sizing positions correctly, both in terms of adding when the thesis is being corroborated and cutting when the thesis is being refuted or kind of asset prices are kind of getting ahead of where we think they should be.

Paulo Baia: Thank you for that. Picking up on the same subject, uh, I'm going to jump to you because, as I mentioned, you've been managing uh, money for outside investors since 2016. Your fund just turned 10 years old. In the crypto space, it's an eternity. So congratulations to you for having been doing this. But, let me tell you one thing. Uh, your strategy is mostly buy and hold. You do also very strong research and you have a very buy and hold strategy there. Uh, but the crypto market has evolved since you started uh, you know, in 2016. Today you can really hedge, you can create a portfolio that's more akin to a traditional hedge fund. Uh, can you tell us about uh, have you evolved your strategy? Because, again, if I go to your website, when I perused your websites before uh, we came here today, if you were an initial investor with him on day one, uh, you're up 60x. Which is fantastic. But not everybody got there on day one. So people are still getting in. So tell me about uh, how you uh, manage money to uh, provide outsized, outsized returns.

Justin Bons: Yeah, I think, I think a lot of it is um, as, as Sam also said, it's, it's research, it's, it's understanding. And there's a lot of opportunity in cryptocurrency that comes also from information asymmetry. Where there is the um, public perception and there is reality. And I think for a large part, for researchers and for investors, it's actually to figure out what reality is. And I think that's a very important aspect and to actually have conviction in that vision. To actually create a thesis that is uh, a sensible, internally consistent thesis. And obviously that thesis needs to constantly evolve. And I think this is also comes down to the psychology of an investor. We have to be able to change our minds. We have to be able to evolve with the times. You know, when I first got into crypto in 2013, I was a Bitcoiner because I, I, I loved Satoshi's writing, I loved what it said in the whitepaper. That was fantastic because I, I believe in on-chain scalability. I think that's, that's really a, a key aspect of, of money and like actually building utility and building real products. So, so that's why I also got into crypto because I can see if we build real products, if we actually affect real change, we can really change the world. And that's also where the most opportunity I think lies as well. It's, it's not just about pure speculation. We can actually identify utility, we can identify revenue. And then as we evolve over time, um, Bitcoin decided to pivot and not scale, so then I moved to Ethereum. And then I was this big Ethereum advocate. And then Ethereum changed its mind and, and Ethereum is no longer scaling, they're going the L2 routes, as, as most of you know here. So then Solana became my new home. Um, and I hope I don't have to move again, uh, but I might have to. And I think that's important. I think it's important to have um, loyalty to, to common sense. To have loyalty to reason and logic and to your thesis. To never have loyalty to a three-letter ticker. Because that's, that's tribalism. That's, that's a trap that I think a lot of people fall into crypto because it's their peer group, it's their community, and then they get tunnel vision. They can't, they can't see clearly anymore. That's why it's always important to always look to your competitors. Like I, I might be a Bitcoin critic, but I, but I spend a lot of time learning about Bitcoin because I have to be sure about that thesis and thorough. And I think, I think to be an investor is to understand. If you understand something, confidence comes from understanding. If you invest in something that you don't understand, it's gambling. And, and this is what separates the investors from the gamblers and the speculators. It's to have conviction. And this is also why we are uh, long-term buy and hold as well. Because it's, it's, it's within that frame that we can actually let that conviction play out. We can tell our investors, you know, we recommend three to five years because the cycle is unpredictable. I mean, I'm a, I'm a fundamental analyst, but the reality is is that fundamentals can predict very little in the short and medium term. And this is where the long term becomes important. And that's where you need patience. Even when drawdowns are massive, don't let that shake your confidence.

Paulo Baia: All right. Let's move on. Uh, let's talk about something very important. Uh, Sam, fundamentals. We were talking about here in the, in the green room and, do fundamentals really matter in crypto? Uh, because right now you're seeing a lot of people calling for, perhaps you should analyze crypto pretty much akin to what you do in the traditional financial market with the Discount Cash Flow analysis and things like that. Or crypto still should be valued on uh, in the growth of network effects at this point?

Sam Andrew: So in, in two words, it, it depends. Let me elaborate on that. Um, so it depends where kind of uh, you are on the spectrum. You being an asset. So at one end of the spectrum we've got memecoins, and there fundamentals do not matter at all. And no one's fooling themselves at that. And somewhere in the middle of the spectrum, we've got infrastructure. L1s, L2s, and various other infrastructure providers. And for infrastructure providers, fundamentals matter a little bit. They matter in the form of the trajectory of those fundamentals. Particularly things like REV, which is kind of like the fees all paid on, on the network. Um, but they don't matter as much in so far as like fundamental like valuation multiples. And that's where I think L1s are more valued on a relative value basis. We can kind of elaborate a little bit more on that. And then at the other end of the spectrum are applications. Think of that primarily DeFi applications. And my view is that the further you are away from the L1, the more the application starts to resemble a business. There are real users, there are revenues, there are costs. And there fundamentals matter a lot. Right? That's revenues, that's fees, and also the multiples at which these applications, protocols trade at, people also pay very close attention to. Now, part of the continuous debate is like there is a problem with fundamentals in crypto, and I can elaborate on that. Which is, the fundamentals are very reflexive. That's a primary problem. Right? And what we see, if SOL price is going up, then the revenues and the kind of KPIs people look at at these applications starts going up. Meanwhile, the underlying hasn't really changed much. Right? And that reflexivity, think of that as like, that's akin to say like Apple's revenue going up just because its stock price is going up. Right? And like that doesn't make sense. And that gets further compounded by airdrops and points farming and things like that. And the second issue we have with fundamentals in crypto is that people extrapolate a lot. So we have nearly like real-time data on how all this stuff is doing and all of a sudden it hits an inflection point and people think that's going to like hockey stick to the moon. And the third issue is uh, leverage. People use a lot of leverage in trading in crypto. So those three compounding effects, what that results in is token prices generally overshoot on where fundamentals roughly are, and then they massively correct off the back of that. And that's what perpetuates part of this crypto cycle.

Paulo Baia: So, uh, I was going to ask you a different question, but we had dinner last night and we were talking about it. So picking up on the same uh, topic here. You have to invest, you talked about the cycles, the three to five year cycles, and uh, that's why you're buy and hold and that's the best way to uh, provide outsized returns to, to investors. But you also have to match that with the liquidity terms of your fund. And usually funds don't have three to five years liquidity terms. So how do you manage that in order to get your investors to buy into this?

Justin Bons: I think, I think because it's challenging. I mean, obviously my fund has been, you know, heavily invested in the Solana ecosystem over the last few years. Um, and I think what is most important is actually selling an investor on the vision. Because it's, it's actually the vision of the future that I think people are ultimately buying into. Because, you know, you're absolutely correct in that if you look at like things like real evaluation, nothing really makes sense. So we have to look at things on a comparative basis. And I think the vision is so grand, as uh, as Raoul also uh, presented earlier, where like crypto actually has the potential to replace most of finance. And I think after that potentially also all of money. This is a profound vision here. I mean this is, this is much larger than I think most people even realize. And, and, and going back to my earlier point, like if, if you, if you know that, if you understand that, that allows you to sleep well at night because you can have confidence in that vision. I think it's very important to impart that on the investors as well. To get them to understand, to understand what they're signing up for. You know, we, we never tell our investors guaranteed return or anything like that. No, it's highly volatile, highly risky. But if you believe in cryptocurrency, if you believe in that long-term vision, then, then this is the greatest opportunity of a lifetime. Especially considering the uh, the disconnect as well between uh, a lot of popular narratives and, and reality. Because that's where the opportunity also lies for investors like us.

Paulo Baia: It's definitely been a great opportunity of a lifetime for your uh, initial investors. But anyways, uh, picking up on that, uh, Sam, uh, let's talk about drawdowns. Because I think one of the most important thing for you to provide outsized returns as a fund manager, it is to manage drawdowns. Because we know crypto can have like 30, 40, 50% drawdowns in a month. We saw it twice this year. We saw it in February and we saw it now in November. So, how do you do, how do you go about it because sometimes uh, you, these days as I mentioned before, crypto market has evolved, you can hedge, uh, people can move their net exposure back and forth. So how do you manage those drawdowns so that you are able to provide those outsized returns?

Sam Andrew: Yeah, so we try and avoid those drawdowns. Right? A, listen, like an 80% drawdown is in most cases like a career ender. A CEO of a company whose stock dropped 80%, like that CEO is fired. Right? A fund manager who experiences something similar, like, they're going to find their way to the door pretty quickly. Um, and uh, to put that kind of in context of what forms a lot of my thinking, my kind of very formative uh, kind of investing career prior to crypto was at a hedge fund called BlueMountain Capital, which was a 22 billion dollar hedge fund, and I started as a credit analyst. And kind of schooled in the thinking of Howard Marks of really understanding where you are in cycles. Right? And he's written a lot about this kind of stuff. And I think the kind of economic and credit cycle that Howard Marks talks about a lot is actually very relevant in crypto. Right? And we see similar things happen through cycles, and that is the kind of euphoric narrative peaks, we have valuations get out of whack, and you every time you have leverage hit new all-time highs. And so those things are actually fairly trackable. Um, and that informs a lot of our thinking on kind of where we are in the cycle. Now, the difference with the kind of credit and economic cycles that Howard Marks alludes to that he says over his roughly 50-year career he's experienced kind of five times, in crypto it happens much faster. It happens much faster and far more frequently. And that's because of those three dynamics I outlined kind of in my prior observation, which was the reflexivity, the extrapolation, and the leverage. So really understanding kind of where you are in that cycle and making sure the risk in the portfolio is commensurate with where we are in that cycle is very important to managing uh, the drawdowns that are unfortunately fairly typical in crypto.

Paulo Baia: So just for the benefit of the audience, just, what do you make of the recent rout in the market? What happened?

Sam Andrew: So, simplistically what happened is in the kind of spring to summer, uh, you had a run-up in asset prices, particularly ETH and SOL, driven primarily by the DATs. Right? DATs were buying a huge amount of both assets. At its peak, 2 billion dollars worth of ETH was being bought by the DATs. And that drove price appreciation where like underlying things that were going on chain hadn't actually changed materially. The other thing we observed is as price goes up, leverage tends to go up as well. And so leverage across the system started peaking. And that creates the kindling for some form of market correction. And you can generally observe the kindling before, and, but understanding what causes the spark is very hard to assess. And in this case the spark was a tweet by Trump on a Friday afternoon around 5 o'clock Eastern Standard that said we're going to put 100% tariffs on China, and that just happened to coincide with a glitch on Binance that caused like massive deleveraging all of a sudden. That's what happened. Uh, and as a result of that, market makers and traders generally lost a lot of money and liquidity evaporated. And that's what caused additional volatility in underlying prices. We saw it in Bitcoin and we saw it across all other alt spectrums. Um, and that's what caused a roughly 50% reduction in the amount of leverage and a 50% drawdown in kind of alt indices. Uh, and that's roughly where we are today.

Paulo Baia: But managing for that kind of drawdown, what happened on October 10th is very difficult for you guys. It's something that came out of the blue.

Sam Andrew: Yeah, so the, no one foresaw the Trump, you know, the Trump tweet and the Binance glitch, but you could foresee the amount of leverage increasing and just the dramatic price appreciation you had in a very short period in asset prices.

Paulo Baia: All right. So we have a few more minutes to go. Let's end with a specific question about specific tokens. Uh, Hype. I know we talked about Hype. Hype has uh, stolen the narrative this year. It's been the token that everybody wanted to own. And uh, we're talking about that perhaps Hype's perps have pretty much eaten up on the uh, let's say the dominance of Solana's revenues. But you believe that uh, Hype has the upper hand right now, but Solana, and we're here at the Solana Breakpoint, can flip that very soon. Can you explain that and how do you position that in your portfolio?

Justin Bons: Absolutely. And this is also where the middle hits the road when we're talking about research and fundamentals. So in particular, uh, Hype has really challenged uh, Solana in terms of revenue. And specifically, it's fair to say that Hype was able to do something that Solana was not. Which was a central limit order book. Um, which ended up being immensely popular uh, because of its speed, uh, because of the ability to do cancellation orders. And, and if we're being really honest with ourselves, Solana is just not capable of doing this today. So this is a real challenge, this is a real uh, there's some real competition for Solana, something we need to take very seriously. Something that thankfully the Solana community does take very seriously. And the reason why I don't think it will last, per se, is that if you, if you look at Hype as an L1, it doesn't exactly match up to Solana. So therefore we have this application that, that lives in um, isolation, that is therefore not composable with a larger DeFi ecosystem. Now the good news here is Alpenglow. Alpenglow, by reducing the latency down to uh, around 150 milliseconds, and also at the same time uh, uh, giving the ability to create cancellation orders, that allows us now to actually build a Hype-type perp exchange on Solana. And I think that is huge. And that is actually where we can see Solana taking back that, that dominance because we can actually uh, clearly see from like a technological perspective where that's going. Because Hype is um, very centralized compared to something like Solana. Uh, Hype, Hype is, is not particularly capable as an L1. And I think this, this vision and this thesis, I talked earlier about having a consistent thesis, is very important. I think composability is, is absolutely critical. Because that's what creates the network effects.

Paulo Baia: So how do you fit that into your portfolio? I know you have a large position in Solana, did you invest in Hype but also at the same time, how do you manage that uh, for investors?

Justin Bons: Absolutely. Yeah. I mean we have a large position in Solana, but we also have a position in Hype. I mean we always need to somewhat hedge our bets and our, and our convictions because the revenue is, is undeniable, the success is undeniable. So, so, so as an investor, we, we have the ability to diversify and, and, and hedge our bets there. Not, not too much because conviction is important, right? But, but in, in our case, we can clearly understand the timeline and we can actually in advance invest in projects such as for example Drift, that will be able to leverage the um, advantages that Alpenglow actually brings. And this is something to be immensely excited about because there's not many other blockchains that actually have this capability. One of the few that I'm thinking about is something like Sui, for example, uh, potentially also has this capability and is a little bit ahead of Solana in that regard. But Solana clearly has the lead in terms of these network effects. And I really do believe if you look at the history of crypto, at least in terms of usage, like Ethereum in terms of usage and revenue overtook Bitcoin because it was 10 times better. And uh, Solana overtook Ethereum because I think it was 10 times better. So I think for anything to overtake Solana now, it needs to be 10 times better. And I don't think Hype is that right now. And, and it's hard to actually identify something that is. And for that reason we can have a lot of confidence in the thesis. And part of the reason why I'm so happy to be here today and to talk to all of you because this is the future of finance and money.

Paulo Baia: All right. With that, uh, our time is up. Uh, I thank you uh, everybody very much for being here. I hope you enjoyed the conversation. I encourage all of you to perhaps go to their website, their websites, North Island uh, Ventures, uh, Cyber Capital, or also grab them, they're going to be walking around here during the day. Uh, thank you very much for being here.

enrichment.generated_query: North Island Ventures Solana Breakpoint 2025 Official Announcement Blog Technical Docs

tags.impact_reason: A general panel discussion on hedge fund strategies where Solana is briefly mentioned as a portfolio asset, but no specific announcements or technical updates are made.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a mountain range composed of crystalline data structures as the Semantic Anchor because it directly visualizes the 'Alpenglow' upgrade's name while representing the theme of technological infrastructure and competitive dominance ('climbing the mountain'). The diegetic text '<1s' was integrated as a holographic HUD element because 'sub-second latency' is the core technological goal driving this institutional pivot.
visual_idea.description: A tight, macro shot focusing on the summit of the crystalline data-mountain. The camera angle looks up at a sharp, geometric peak made of interwoven glass-like data shards and micro-circuitry. The 'alpenglow' light hits the faceted surfaces, creating intense golden-orange highlights and casting deep, obsidian-like shadows. The intricate details of the technological 'rock' are visible, emphasizing the complexity and power of the underlying infrastructure.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A tight, macro shot focusing on the summit of the crystalline data-mountain. The camera angle looks up at a sharp, geometric peak made of interwoven glass-like data shards and micro-circuitry. The 'alpenglow' light hits the faceted surfaces, creating intense golden-orange highlights and casting deep, obsidian-like shadows. The intricate details of the technological 'rock' are visible, emphasizing the complexity and power of the underlying infrastructure. Crystalline Data Structures, Polished Obsidian textures, Alpenglow Lighting with warm amber reflections and cinematic golden-hour tones, Digital Everest aesthetic. Symmetrical, centrally focused, tight macro framing, filling the entire 1:1 square aspect ratio with texture. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic, high dynamic range, cinematic contrast.

editorial.headline: Solana’s Alpenglow Upgrade Targets Hyperliquid’s Perpetual Dominance
editorial.standfirst: Institutional allocators signal a strategic pivot as Solana’s sub-second latency upgrade aims to reclaim lost derivatives revenue from app-chain competitors. The thesis shifts from passive holding to active infrastructure plays.
key_takeaways: **Alpenglow** reduces Solana latency to ~150ms and adds cancellation orders, enabling CLOBs to rival **Hyperliquid**.
**North Island Ventures** and **Cyber Capital** emphasize active risk management following the recent 50% market drawdown triggered by US-China tariff tensions.
Solana-native derivatives protocols like **Drift** are positioned to recapture market share through superior composability network effects.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The narrative that app-chains are the inevitable endgame for DeFi derivatives faces its first existential test. **Hyperliquid** (Hype) has successfully siphoned liquidity and revenue from general-purpose L1s by offering a high-performance Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) that legacy Solana architecture could not match. However, the window for this arbitrage is closing. The introduction of **Alpenglow** represents a paradigm shift, reducing Solana’s latency to approximately 150 milliseconds and, crucially, enabling native cancellation orders. This technical unlock neutralizes the speed advantage of specialized chains while retaining the liquidity benefits of a monolithic state.

For institutional allocators, this creates a clear rotation signal. **Cyber Capital** CIO **Justin Bons** argues that while **Hyperliquid** currently holds the revenue crown, its isolation as a specialized L1 limits the network effects that drive long-term value. The "Alpha" trade is not merely chasing current yield, but positioning for the composability premium. When **Drift** and other Solana-native perp DEXs integrate **Alpenglow**, they will offer the same high-frequency trading experience as **Hyperliquid** but within a unified liquidity layer, effectively rendering the app-chain thesis for derivatives obsolete.

This infrastructure thesis, however, demands rigorous risk management. The recent market rout—precipitated by a 100% China tariff announcement from the Trump administration—erased 50% of altcoin leverage overnight, punishing passive exposure. **North Island Ventures** Portfolio Manager **Sam Andrew** notes that while the industry has entered a "Golden Age of Crypto Adoption," the market remains violently reflexive. The winners of this cycle will not be passive holders, but active managers who can distinguish between genuine infrastructure innovation and leverage-fueled price action.
